content_markdown:
A fixed mass of an ideal gas has a constant volume. Two quantities, $R$ and $S$, of the gas vary as shown by the graph below.

What quantities do $R$ and $S$ represent?

diagram_metadata.description: A linear graph showing quantity R on the vertical axis and quantity S on the horizontal axis. The line has a positive gradient and a negative y-intercept. The line crosses the S-axis at a positive value of S.

options.label: A
options.text: R: pressure; S: temperature in kelvin

options.label: B
options.text: R: pressure; S: temperature in degree Celsius

options.label: C
options.text: R: temperature in kelvin; S: pressure

options.label: D
options.text: R: temperature in degree Celsius; S: pressure

vibe_explanation: According to the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$, at constant volume, pressure is directly proportional to absolute temperature (Kelvin). A graph of $P$ vs $T$ in Kelvin would pass through the origin $(0,0)$. However, the graph shown has a negative y-intercept. If $R$ is pressure and $S$ is temperature in Celsius, the relationship is $P \propto (T_{Celsius} + 273.15)$, which results in a positive x-intercept (at $-273.15^\circ\text{C}$) or a negative y-intercept if the axes are shifted. Conversely, if $R$ is temperature in Celsius and $S$ is pressure, $T_{Celsius} = \frac{V}{nR}P - 273.15$, which matches a linear graph with a negative y-intercept.
